title: Why We Love Being Scared
subtitle: 明明害怕还要看恐怖片？人类享受恐惧的秘密

paragraphs.en: Imagine you're an alien sent to Earth to document the behaviour of the mammals inhabiting the planet. You stumble into a movie theatre that's showing the latest Hollywood horror film. Several dozen humans are gathered together in a dark, undecorated room. They're all staring at a rectangular area on which patterns of light change rapidly. They are clearly in a state of high arousal. Their heart rate is elevated, they occasionally glance around nervously, and they sometimes jump collectively in their seats, emitting high-pitched warning calls.
paragraphs.zh: 想象你是个被派到地球记录哺乳动物行为的外星人。你无意中走进一家正在放映最新好莱坞恐怖片的电影院。几十个人聚集在一间漆黑、毫无装饰的房间里。他们都盯着一个矩形区域，上面的光影图案快速变化。他们显然处于高度唤醒状态。心率加快，时而紧张地环顾四周，时而集体从座位上跳起，发出尖锐的警告声。

paragraphs.en: Eventually, the lights come up and the rectangular screen goes black. The humans stand up and leave the room, chatting and laughing, and showing signs of pleasure. Why do these humans voluntarily expose themselves to what appears to be a deeply unpleasant experience? And why do they react so strongly to those patterns of light on a screen? I am fascinated with the paradox of horror — the strange fact that many people seek out scary entertainment. I think the answer lies in human nature.
paragraphs.zh: 最后灯光亮起，矩形屏幕变黑。人类站起来有说有笑地离开房间，脸上带着愉悦。为什么这些人自愿让自己经历看似非常不愉快的体验？为什么他们对屏幕上那些光影图案反应如此强烈？我对恐怖的悖论着迷——许多人主动寻求恐怖娱乐这一奇怪事实。我认为答案在于人性。

paragraphs.en: My research suggests that we humans evolved to find pleasure in situations that allow us to experience negative emotions in a safe context. You can see these elements of horror in children's games. Take hide-and-seek, which is a simulation of a predator-prey interaction. The kid hides and the adult pretends to be a predator, searching for the child while howling like a dangerous beast. This simulation gives the child crucial information about how to avoid becoming prey, and children tend to find that kind of activity deeply satisfying.
paragraphs.zh: 我的研究表明，人类进化到能在安全环境中体验负面情绪的情境中找到愉悦。你可以在儿童游戏中看到这些恐怖元素。以捉迷藏为例，它是对捕食者-猎物互动的模拟。孩子躲藏，大人假装捕食者，一边像危险野兽般嚎叫一边寻找孩子。这种模拟为孩子提供了如何避免成为猎物的重要信息，而孩子们往往觉得这类活动非常令人满足。

paragraphs.en: They find it pleasurable, and pleasure is evolution's way of motivating us toward adaptive behaviour. Horror is pleasurable to many people because it lets us play with negative emotions and develop coping strategies. We learn what it feels like to be truly afraid, and we learn how to handle negative emotion. How, then, does horror work? My research suggests that horror works by exploiting an ancient set of biological defence mechanisms — an evolved fear system, which we share with other animals.
paragraphs.zh: 他们觉得这很愉悦，而愉悦是进化激励我们采取适应性行为的方式。恐怖对许多人来说是愉悦的，因为它让我们把玩负面情绪并发展应对策略。我们了解真正恐惧的感觉，学习如何处理负面情绪。那么恐怖是如何运作的？我的研究表明，恐怖通过利用一套古老的生物防御机制——一种我们与其他动物共有的进化恐惧系统——来发挥作用。

paragraphs.en: But humans are uniquely imaginative, and we use our evolved imagination to travel into virtual worlds that are full of danger. There are good reasons for watching a horror film, even if you're not a loyal horror fan. If you make it through the film in one piece, you'll probably experience a strong sense of mastery, a sense that you were able to make it through an appalling experience. Anyway, watching a horror film makes you better at handling your own fear, and who knows when that will become critically relevant?
paragraphs.zh: 但人类具有独特的想象力，我们利用进化的想象力进入充满危险的虚拟世界。观看恐怖片有充分的理由，即使你不是忠实的恐怖片迷。如果你完整看完影片，可能会体验到强烈的掌控感，一种你能够熬过可怕经历的感觉。无论如何，看恐怖片能让你更好地处理自己的恐惧，谁知道这何时会变得至关重要呢？
